records.ety: [F. redresser to straighten; pref. re- re- + dresser to raise, arrange. See Dress.]

defs: To put in order again; to set right; to emend; to revise.
quotes: The common profit could she redress.
In yonder spring of roses intermixed With myrtle, find what to redress till noon.
Your wish that I should redress a certain paper which you had prepared.

defs: To set right, as a wrong; to repair, as an injury; to make amends for; to remedy; to relieve from.
quotes: Those wrongs, those bitter injuries, . . . I doubt not but with honor to redress.

defs: To make amends or compensation to; to relieve of anything unjust or oppressive; to bestow relief upon.
quotes: Will Gaul or Muscovite redress ye?

defs: The act of redressing; a making right; reformation; correction; amendment.
quotes: Reformation of evil laws is commendable, but for us the more necessary is a speedy redress of ourselves.

defs: A setting right, as of wrong, injury, or opression; as, the redress of grievances; hence, relief; remedy; reparation; indemnification.
quotes: A few may complain without reason; but there is occasion for redress when the cry is universal.

defs: One who, or that which, gives relief; a redresser.
quotes: Fair majesty, the refuge and redress Of those whom fate pursues and wants oppress.
